ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean fried carrot cake (chai tow kway). FOODON_03311069 (fried food product) is used as the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chai tow kway or fried carrot cake. A0CKP (stir-fried rice-based dish) is used as the closest defensible parent. The dish is made from steamed radish rice cake stir-fried with egg, preserved radish, and luncheon meat.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept exists for this specific Singaporean hawker dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker plate of luncheon meat fried carrot cake weighs approximately 300 g, comprising stir-fried radish rice cake cubes, sliced luncheon meat, egg, and preserved radish.

nutrient_source.title: Fried Carrot Cake (Chai Tow Kway) — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo provides nutrient data for plain fried carrot cake (chai tow kway). Values for luncheon meat fried carrot cake are adapted by incorporating the nutritional contribution of luncheon meat (USDA FoodData Central NDB 07042, canned luncheon meat) proportionally into the base dish. The adaptation increases protein, fat, saturated fat, and sodium relative to the plain version. Energy and macros are recalculated accordingly. This is an estimated adaptation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice cake and steamed rice-based products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for luncheon meat fried carrot cake. The GI of 55 is estimated by analogy with steamed and stir-fried rice cake products (GI range 50–61) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The addition of fat and protein from luncheon meat and egg is expected to moderately attenuate the glycemic response relative to plain rice cake. GI 55 (low) is used as a conservative central estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (55 × 59.4) / 100 ≈ 32.7, rounded to 32.9 using adapted per-100g carbohydrate of 19.8 g × 300 g serving / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from luncheon meat and preserved radish
hypertension.guidance: At approximately 620 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1860 mg per standard 300 g serving), this dish provides more than 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals and should be consumed only occasionally and in reduced portions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index but high glycemic load per serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Despite a low GI of approximately 55, the glycemic load of approximately 33 per standard serving is high. Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard plate and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables to manage postprandial glucose response effectively.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, sodium, and cholesterol from luncheon meat and egg
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of saturated fat (approximately 3.2 g per 100 g), high sodium (approximately 620 mg per 100 g), and dietary cholesterol (approximately 72 mg per 100 g) from luncheon meat and egg makes this dish a high-concern food for individuals with cardiovascular disease, who should restrict intake to occasional small servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Luncheon meat fried carrot cake (午餐肉菜頭粿) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish that is a variation of the classic chai tow kway (fried carrot cake). Despite its name, the dish contains no carrot; the 'carrot cake' refers to steamed radish rice cake (made from shredded white radish and rice flour), which is cut into cubes and stir-fried in a wok with egg, preserved radish (chai poh), garlic, spring onion, and cooking oil. In this luncheon meat variant, sliced or diced canned luncheon meat is added, contributing a salty, savory, and slightly smoky flavour profile. The dish is available in two styles: 'white' (without dark soy sauce) and 'black' (with dark soy sauce, which adds sweetness and colour). The texture is a contrast of crispy outer edges on the rice cake cubes with a soft interior, combined with the firm, slightly fatty texture of luncheon meat and the silkiness of scrambled egg. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 185 kcal per 100 g, with moderate carbohydrate (19.8 g), moderate fat (9.5 g), and moderate protein (6.8 g). The sodium content is high at approximately 620 mg per 100 g due to the luncheon meat and preserved radish, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 55 (low), but the glycemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 33 (high), which is clinically significant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is a staple of Singapore hawker culture and is commonly consumed as a breakfast or lunch item.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo data for plain fried carrot cake (chai tow kway) with proportional adjustment for luncheon meat contribution using USDA FoodData Central data. No exact HPB entry for luncheon meat fried carrot cake was found. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using steamed rice cake products as the reference. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: 1. The food_group in the input JSON was listed as 'Desserts', which is incorrect for this savoury hawker dish. It has been corrected to 'Mixed' as it is a stir-fried rice cake dish with meat and egg. 2. Nutrient values are adapted estimates combining HPB Foodo plain chai tow kway data with USDA FoodData Central luncheon meat data (NDB 07042). 3. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 4. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 5. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo source database for this dish category. 6. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on steamed and stir-fried rice cake products from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct measurement for this specific dish exists. This GI value of 55 classifies the dish as 'low' GI. 7. Halal status is marked false as luncheon meat typically contains pork; halal-certified luncheon meat variants exist but are not assumed by default. 8. The dish is also known as 'carrot cake' in Singapore hawker culture, which can cause confusion with the Western dessert of the same name — the Singapore version contains no carrot and is a savoury dish.
